words.shortDefinition: Transmission Control Protocol; a reliable, connection-oriented protocol.
words.simpleDefinition: A reliable, connection-oriented protocol for exchanging data — ensures every packet arrives intact, requests retransmission for lost pieces, and maintains strict delivery order. Essential for applications like web browsing and file transfers where data integrity is paramount.
words.longDefinition: One of the main protocols of the Internet protocol suite. It provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of a stream of octets (bytes) between applications running on hosts communicating via an IP network.
words.connector: Think of TCP like sending a registered letter — you receive a signature confirmation, and the post office re-sends any lost mail.
words.techContext: TCP is used for applications where data integrity is paramount, such as web browsing (HTTP), email (SMTP), and file transfer (FTP).
words.exampleSentence: The application experienced high latency because of the TCP three-way handshake required for each new connection.

words.shortDefinition: User Datagram Protocol; a simple, connectionless communication protocol.
words.simpleDefinition: A high-speed, connectionless protocol prioritizing rapid transmission over guaranteed delivery — packets are fired off without waiting for acknowledgments. Ideal for time-sensitive applications like video streaming or online gaming where dropping some data is better than waiting.
words.longDefinition: Unlike TCP, UDP does not guarantee delivery, order, or error-checking. It is used for time-sensitive applications where dropping a few packets is preferable to waiting for retransmissions.
words.connector: Think of UDP like shouting across a room — you speak once, fast, and don't repeat yourself if the listener misses a word.
words.techContext: We used UDP for our real-time video streaming service to minimize lag, even if it meant occasional frame drops.
words.exampleSentence: Online gaming and VOIP services primarily use UDP because low latency is more important than perfect data reliability.

words.shortDefinition: A security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic.
words.simpleDefinition: Security system monitoring and controlling incoming and outgoing traffic based on predefined safety rules — creating a protective barrier between trusted and untrusted networks. Essential for blocking unauthorized access and preventing cyberattacks from reaching internal systems.
words.longDefinition: Establishes a barrier between a trusted network and an untrusted network (like the Internet). It uses a set of security rules to decide whether to allow or block specific traffic.
words.connector: Think of a firewall like a bouncer at a club — they check every person against the guest list and turn away anyone unauthorized.
words.techContext: We had to open a hole in the firewall for port 8080 to allow our new API to be reachable from the outside world.
words.exampleSentence: The firewall blocked the attempted attack because it detected a series of suspicious connection requests from an unknown source.
words.etymology: Metaphor from physical firewalls designed to stop the spread of fire between buildings.

words.shortDefinition: The time delay between a request and its response.
words.simpleDefinition: Time delay between a request and its response — measuring how long a user waits for a system to process their action. Network, database, and disk I/O all contribute to the total perceived latency of a request.
words.longDefinition: Latency measures how long it takes to perform a single operation. Lower latency means faster response times. Network latency, database query latency, and disk I/O latency all contribute to overall system latency.
words.connector: Think of latency like the pause between flicking a light switch and the bulb actually lighting up — shorter feels instant, longer feels broken.
words.techContext: Critical for user experience. High latency makes systems feel slow even with high throughput. Often confused with throughput.
words.exampleSentence: We reduced API latency from 500ms to 50ms by adding a Redis cache in front of the database.
words.etymology: from Latin latere ("to lie hidden") — the hidden delay in a system.

codeExample.code:
# Measure latency:
curl -w "Total time: %{time_total}s\n" https://api.example.com

# Output: Total time: 0.234s

words.shortDefinition: Open Systems Interconnection model; a conceptual framework for networking.
words.simpleDefinition: A conceptual seven-layer framework standardizing how different systems communicate — from physical cables (Layer 1) to user applications (Layer 7). Helps engineers troubleshoot connectivity issues by isolating problems within specific, functional layers of the network stack.
words.longDefinition: Consists of seven layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application. It helps standardize the functions of a telecommunication system without regard to its underlying internal structure and technology.
words.connector: Think of the OSI Model like a restaurant — customer interacts with the waiter, who talks to the kitchen, which uses equipment — each role is separate.
words.techContext: When troubleshooting a connection issue, we often start at Layer 1 (Physical) and work our way up to Layer 7 (Application).
words.exampleSentence: The router operates at Layer 3 of the OSI model, focusing on IP addresses and routing.

words.shortDefinition: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol; automatically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated network service handing out unique IP addresses and configuration parameters to devices as they connect — eliminating the need for manual setup. Simplifies administration by dynamically managing a pool of addresses for temporary leases.
words.longDefinition: A network management protocol used on Internet Protocol (IP) networks for automatically assigning IP addresses and other communication parameters to devices connected to the network using a client–server architecture.
words.connector: Think of DHCP like a hotel check-in desk — when you arrive, they hand you a room key without you picking the number yourself.
words.techContext: Your home router typically acts as a DHCP server, giving your laptop and phone their local IP addresses automatically.
words.exampleSentence: The laptop failed to connect to the WiFi because it couldn't reach the DHCP server to get an IP address.
words.etymology: Acronym for Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol.

words.shortDefinition: Transport Layer Security; provides authentication and encryption over a network.
words.simpleDefinition: Security protocol providing end-to-end encryption and authentication for network communications — protecting data from eavesdropping and tampering. Modern successor to SSL, it establishes the secure 'HTTPS' connections required for safe web browsing and API interactions.
words.longDefinition: The successor to SSL (Secure Sockets Layer). It encrypts the data sent between a web browser and a server, ensuring that the communication is private and hasn't been tampered with. It is the 'S' in HTTPS.
words.connector: Think of TLS like a sealed tamper-evident envelope — anyone can see the letter's box, but nobody can read or change the contents.
words.techContext: We upgraded our server to support TLS 1.3 to ensure the highest level of security and faster connection speeds.
words.exampleSentence: The browser showed a certificate warning because the server was using an expired TLS certificate.

words.shortDefinition: Secure Shell; a cryptographic network protocol for operating network services securely over an unsecured network.
words.simpleDefinition: Secure, encrypted protocol for remote computer access and command execution — enabling administrators to manage servers over untrusted networks safely. It replaces older, insecure methods by establishing a cryptographic tunnel that protects login credentials and session data.
words.longDefinition: Most notably used for remote login and command-line execution. It provides a secure channel over an insecure network in a client-server architecture, connecting an SSH client application with an SSH server.
words.connector: Think of SSH like a private, soundproofed phone line — everything you say is scrambled so no eavesdropper can understand your conversation.
words.techContext: I used SSH to log into the production server and manually restart the web service.
words.exampleSentence: To improve security, we disabled password-based SSH and now only allow logins using cryptographic keys.

words.shortDefinition: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol; the standard for electronic mail transmission.
words.simpleDefinition: The standard protocol for transmitting outgoing email between servers — acting as the digital postal service for electronic messages. It handles the initial relay of mail from the client to the recipient's mailbox server.
words.longDefinition: A protocol used by mail servers and other message transfer agents to send and receive mail messages. While it is primarily used for sending mail, other protocols like IMAP or POP3 are used for retrieving it.
words.connector: Think of SMTP like a mail sorting office — it takes your letter and hands it to the right carrier for final delivery.
words.techContext: We integrated an SMTP relay service to ensure our application's transactional emails don't end up in users' spam folders.
words.exampleSentence: The email notification failed because our application couldn't connect to the SMTP server on port 587.
words.etymology: Acronym for Simple Mail Transfer Protocol.

words.shortDefinition: Encapsulates access to an external system behind a simplified interface.
words.simpleDefinition: Object encapsulating access to external systems — providing a simplified interface while hiding complex networking or protocol details. Gateways act as a protective barrier, making external dependencies easier to mock and test in isolation.
words.longDefinition: Wraps the API of an external system with an interface that is easier to use or more suitable for your application. It isolates the complexity of external communication—handling protocols, serialization, and error mapping—and provides a stable point for testing and substitution. Using a gateway allows the application to depend on a local interface rather than being tightly coupled to the specific quirks of a third-party service.
words.connector: Think of a gateway like a hotel concierge — they handle all the complex outside arrangements so you only have to ask one person.
words.techContext: Used to wrap third-party APIs, legacy systems, or external services. Enables easier testing via mocking.
words.exampleSentence: We built a payment gateway to abstract away the differences between Stripe and PayPal.
words.etymology: from gate (Old English geat) + way (Old English weg) — literally "a way through a gate."

words.shortDefinition: An intermediary server that sits between a client and another server.
words.simpleDefinition: Intermediary server sitting between clients and destinations — fetching resources on behalf of the user. Can provide anonymity, content filtering, or performance-enhancing caching while masking the client's original IP address from the final server.
words.longDefinition: It receives requests from clients and forwards them to the destination server. It can be used for security, anonymity, caching, or to bypass regional restrictions. Types include forward proxies and reverse proxies.
words.connector: Think of a proxy server like a personal shopper — you tell them what you want, they get it, and the shop never sees you.
words.techContext: We use Nginx as a reverse proxy to handle SSL termination and load balancing for our Node.js cluster.
words.exampleSentence: I configured my browser to use a corporate proxy server to access internal company resources from home.

words.shortDefinition: A system that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers.
words.simpleDefinition: System distributing incoming network traffic across a pool of servers — ensuring no single machine becomes overwhelmed. It performs regular health checks and uses specific algorithms to route traffic, maximizing application availability and responsiveness during traffic spikes.
words.longDefinition: Load balancers improve availability and scalability by spreading requests across a pool of servers. They perform health checks, route traffic based on algorithms (round-robin, least connections), and provide failover. Operate at Layer 4 (TCP) or Layer 7 (HTTP).
words.connector: Think of a load balancer like the person at a busy bank pointing you to the next free teller — they split the queue evenly.
words.techContext: Fundamental to horizontal scaling. Load balancers enable zero-downtime deploys, A/B testing, and geographic routing.
words.exampleSentence: We put an Application Load Balancer in front of our EC2 instances to distribute traffic and handle SSL termination.
words.etymology: load from Old English lad ("course, way"); balance from Latin bilanx ("having two scales").

codeExample.code:
# ALB configuration:
load_balancer:
  algorithm: round_robin
  targets:
    - server1:8080
    - server2:8080
    - server3:8080
  health_check: /health

words.shortDefinition: Internet Control Message Protocol; used by network devices to send error messages and operational information.
words.simpleDefinition: Supporting protocol used by network devices for diagnostic and error reporting — facilitating pings and traceroutes to verify connectivity. It serves as the feedback mechanism for networks, identifying unreachable hosts or services and explaining transmission failures.
words.longDefinition: A supporting protocol in the Internet protocol suite. It is used by network devices, like routers, to send error messages indicating, for example, that a requested service is not available or that a host or router could not be reached.
words.connector: Think of ICMP like a return-to-sender sticker — when a packet fails delivery, ICMP explains exactly why it was sent back.
words.techContext: The 'ping' and 'traceroute' utilities both use ICMP messages to test network connectivity and path.
words.exampleSentence: I sent an ICMP Echo Request to the server to check if it was still online.
words.etymology: Acronym for Internet Control Message Protocol.

words.shortDefinition: Network Address Translation; allows multiple devices to share a single public IP address.
words.simpleDefinition: Networking method translating private internal IP addresses into a single shared public address — allowing multiple home or office devices to use one internet connection. Helps conserve the limited pool of IPv4 addresses while providing a basic layer of security.
words.longDefinition: A method of remapping an IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit across a traffic routing device.
words.connector: Think of NAT like a company switchboard — all calls arrive on one public number and get routed to the correct internal extension.
words.techContext: Your router uses NAT to allow all the devices in your home to access the internet using the single IP address assigned by your ISP.
words.exampleSentence: We had to set up a NAT Gateway in our VPC to allow our private servers to download security updates from the internet.

words.shortDefinition: A number that defines the range of IP addresses that can be used in a network.
words.simpleDefinition: Numerical filter defining which portion of an IP address refers to the network and which portion refers to the specific device. Used by systems to determine if traffic should stay within the local network or move to a gateway.
words.longDefinition: Used to divide an IP address into two parts: one that identifies the network and one that identifies the host. It helps routers determine if an IP address is in the local network or an external one.
words.connector: Think of a subnet mask like the city vs. street in an address — it separates the broader area from the specific building.
words.techContext: A common subnet mask is 255.255.255.0, which means the first 24 bits are for the network and the last 8 bits are for local devices.
words.exampleSentence: I misconfigured the subnet mask, which prevented the server from communicating with other machines on the same local network.

words.shortDefinition: Round-Trip Time; the time it takes for a signal to go to a destination and back.
words.simpleDefinition: Total time elapsed for a signal to travel from sender to receiver and back again — a key metric for evaluating network responsiveness. Directly impacts protocol performance, especially TCP, which relies on timely acknowledgments to maintain flow.
words.longDefinition: A key measure of network latency. It includes the propagation delay, processing delay, and queuing delay at each node along the path.
words.connector: Think of RTT like clocking a ball throw and catch — measuring the total journey time from your hand and back.
words.techContext: A high RTT is particularly damaging for TCP performance because it slows down the window scaling and congestion control algorithms.
words.exampleSentence: I ran a ping command to measure the RTT between my local machine and the database server.

words.shortDefinition: The most recent version of the Internet Protocol.
words.simpleDefinition: Modern IP version using 128-bit addresses — providing a nearly infinite pool of unique identifiers for devices. It solves the address exhaustion problem of the older 32-bit IPv4 system, enabling the continued growth of the global internet.
words.longDefinition: Designed to replace IPv4, it uses 128-bit addresses (allowing for 3.4×10^38 addresses) compared to IPv4's 32-bit addresses (4.3 billion). This solves the problem of IP address exhaustion.
words.connector: Think of IPv6 like replacing a city's 4-digit phone system with a 12-digit one — suddenly there are enough numbers for everyone.
words.techContext: IPv6 addresses are written in hexadecimal and separated by colons, such as 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334.
words.exampleSentence: We enabled IPv6 on our load balancer to ensure we can support users who are on IPv6-only mobile networks.

words.shortDefinition: Address Resolution Protocol; maps an IP address to a physical MAC address.
words.simpleDefinition: Low-level protocol mapping a known IP address to a physical MAC address — bridging the gap between logical network addressing and physical hardware identity. Essential for delivering data packets to the correct device on a local network segment.
words.longDefinition: Used to find the hardware address (MAC address) of a device from its known IP address. It operates between Layers 2 and 3 of the OSI model.
words.connector: Think of ARP like asking 'who in this room is in seat 14?' — you shout the question and the correct person raises their hand.
words.techContext: When a router wants to send a packet to a local device, it first broadcasts an ARP request to find which MAC address corresponds to that device's IP.
words.exampleSentence: I checked the ARP table on the switch to see which port the new server was connected to.

words.shortDefinition: A method of grouping data into packets that are transmitted over a digital network.
words.simpleDefinition: Networking method breaking data into small chunks (packets) that travel independently through a network to be reassembled at the destination. Allows many devices to share the same communications lines efficiently, forming the backbone of the modern internet.
words.longDefinition: Unlike circuit switching (like old phone lines), packet switching allows many devices to share the same network path by breaking data into small chunks that find their own way to the destination.
words.connector: Think of packet switching like shipping furniture flat-pack — each box takes whatever van is free and you assemble them at the end.
words.techContext: The entire Internet is based on packet switching, which allows for efficient and resilient communication.
words.exampleSentence: Packet switching allows our network to automatically reroute traffic if one of the intermediate routers fails.

words.shortDefinition: Virtual Local Area Network; a logical subnetwork that groups together devices from different physical LANs.
words.simpleDefinition: Logically isolated network created within a physical infrastructure — allowing devices on different switches to communicate as if they were on the same private line. Used to enhance security and improve network management by grouping systems based on function.
words.longDefinition: Allows network administrators to group hosts together even if they are not connected to the same network switch. This improves security and reduces broadcast traffic by isolating groups of devices.
words.connector: Think of a VLAN like colored wristbands at a festival — everyone is in the same field, but wristbands determine which areas they can access.
words.techContext: We put our IP cameras on a separate VLAN to isolate them from the rest of the corporate network for security reasons.
words.exampleSentence: I configured the switch ports to be part of the same VLAN so the servers could communicate as if they were on the same physical cable.
words.etymology: Acronym for Virtual Local Area Network.

words.shortDefinition: Border Gateway Protocol; the protocol that makes the Internet work by routing data between networks.
words.simpleDefinition: The foundational routing protocol of the global internet — enabling large networks (Autonomous Systems) to exchange routing and reachability information. It acts as the internet's master map, determining the most efficient paths for data across thousands of ISPs.
words.longDefinition: An exterior gateway protocol designed to exchange routing and reachability information among autonomous systems (AS) on the Internet. It is responsible for finding the best path for data across the global network.
words.connector: Think of BGP like negotiations between airlines — each advertises its routes, and together they figure out how to get you to your destination.
words.techContext: The massive Facebook outage in 2021 was caused by a BGP configuration error that essentially erased their servers from the Internet's map.
words.exampleSentence: Our ISP is experiencing BGP instability, which is causing intermittent routing issues for our users in South America.

words.shortDefinition: Maximum Transmission Unit; the largest size of a packet that can be sent over a network.
words.simpleDefinition: The largest packet size — measured in bytes — that a specific network link can transmit in a single operation. Packets exceeding this size must be fragmented into smaller pieces, which can negatively impact network performance and reliability.
words.longDefinition: If a packet is larger than the MTU of a network segment, it must be fragmented (broken into smaller pieces), which can degrade performance and cause connectivity issues.
words.connector: Think of MTU like the maximum parcel size a courier accepts — anything bigger must be split into multiple smaller boxes.
words.techContext: The standard MTU for Ethernet is 1500 bytes, but 'Jumbo Frames' can support up to 9000 bytes for better efficiency in data centers.
words.exampleSentence: We had to lower the MTU on our VPN interface because some routers along the path were dropping large encrypted packets.

words.techContext: CIDR is commonly used in professional software development.
words.exampleSentence: We used CIDR in our project.

words.shortDefinition: The number of operations a system can handle per unit of time.
words.simpleDefinition: Total number of operations or requests a system handles per unit of time — indicating overall capacity. Unlike latency which measures individual speed, throughput measures the volume of work a system pushes through under sustained load.
words.longDefinition: Throughput measures system capacity — requests per second, transactions per minute, or bytes per second. High throughput means a system can process many operations simultaneously. Improving throughput often requires horizontal scaling or parallelization.
words.connector: Think of throughput like a checkout line's 'customers per minute' — it measures how many people are served in total each hour.
words.techContext: Key metric for capacity planning. A system can have high throughput but high latency, or low latency but low throughput.
words.exampleSentence: Our API handles 10,000 requests per second at peak, but throughput drops to 2,000 when the database is under load.
words.etymology: from through + put — literally "putting through" work in a given time.

codeExample.code:
# Load test throughput:
ab -n 10000 -c 100 https://api.example.com/

# Requests per second: 2500 [#/sec]
